s2.q: Which of the following is a common postpartum physical problem?
s2.explain: Common postpartum physical problems include: postpartum hemorrhage, infections, breastfeeding issues, perineal pain, pelvic pain, urinary/bowel problems, fatigue and anemia, thyroid disorders, and contraception needs.

s2.q: Postpartum thyroiditis can lead to which condition?
s2.explain: Postpartum thyroiditis can lead to hyper- or hypothyroidism.

s3.q: Which postpartum emotional condition requires urgent psychiatric care?
s3.explain: Postpartum psychosis is rare but severe, presenting with hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized behavior — it requires urgent psychiatric care.

s3.q: What distinguishes postpartum depression (PPD) from baby blues?
options: PPD occurs only in first-time mothers
PPD is more severe and prolonged, affecting ability to care for self or baby
Baby blues last longer than PPD
s3.explain: PPD is more severe and prolonged depressive symptoms affecting the mother's ability to care for herself or her baby, while baby blues are mild and transient.

s4.q: Which of the following is a psychosocial concern during the postpartum period?
options: Social isolation especially if support systems are limited
s4.explain: Psychosocial concerns include: adjustment to motherhood, relationship strain, social isolation, and work/financial stress.

s5.q: How many main postpartum breast problems are covered in the chapter?
s5.explain: Three main postpartum breast problems are covered: breast engorgement, nipple fissure (cracked nipples), and insufficient milk supply.

s6.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s6.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.
Breast engorgement occurs when breasts become overly full, hard, and painful, usually 3-5 days postpartum when milk 'comes in'.

s6.q: Which of the following is a management strategy for breast engorgement?
options: Warm compresses before feeding, cold after
s6.explain: Management includes: frequent breastfeeding/pumping, hand expression to soften areola, warm compresses before feeding and cold after, supportive bra without underwire, and pain relief.

s7.q: What is the most common cause of nipple fissure (cracked nipples)?
s7.explain: Nipple fissure is often due to improper latching, positioning, or excessive moisture.

s7.q: Which of the following is recommended for managing cracked nipples?
options: Stop breastfeeding on the affected side
Lanolin cream or breast milk on nipples
Apply alcohol to dry the area
s7.explain: Management includes: correcting latch/positioning, lanolin cream or breast milk on nipples, air drying after feeds, and nipple shields in severe cases.

s8.q: Which of the following is a cause of insufficient milk supply?
s8.explain: Causes include: poor latch, infrequent feeds, maternal stress, and dehydration.

s8.q: Which intervention helps stimulate milk production in cases of insufficient supply?
s8.explain: Management includes: frequent and effective breastfeeding, skin-to-skin contact to stimulate milk production, balanced diet, good hydration, and use of galactagogues.

s9.q: Which of the following is a preventive measure for postpartum breast problems?
options: Use of underwired bras for support
Limit breastfeeding to 5 minutes per session
s9.explain: Prevention includes: correct latching, frequent feeding, keeping nipples clean and dry, supportive bra without underwire, and balanced diet with hydration.

s10.q: When do baby blues typically begin and how long can they last?
options: Begin immediately after delivery, last up to 1 week
Begin 2-3 days postpartum, last up to 2 weeks
Begin 1 week postpartum, last up to 1 month
Begin 2 weeks postpartum, last up to 6 weeks
s10.explain: Baby blues typically begin 2-3 days postpartum and can last up to 2 weeks. They are mild and resolve on their own.

s10.q: What distinguishes baby blues from postpartum depression?
options: Baby blues are less severe and resolve on their own
Baby blues only affect first-time mothers
s10.explain: Baby blues are less severe than postpartum depression and resolve on their own. If symptoms last longer than 2 weeks or become more intense, it may indicate PPD.

s10.q: Which of the following is the primary cause of baby blues?
options: Hormonal changes (sudden drop in estrogen and progesterone after delivery)
s10.explain: Causes of baby blues include: hormonal changes (sudden drop in estrogen & progesterone after delivery), physical stress of childbirth, emotional factors, and lack of sleep.
